item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point; implemented.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why do all the notes literally say "literally" (I said literally on purpose to prove my point on how it doesn't help whatsoever) in the notes either put only "steel mother" or "translated from german to steel mother"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
to be more clear:

there were a bunch of Magic Miracle Machines that the anomalous artisan made. They're all broken now, except the Stahlmutter. The last one to break was the timeline-crosser: it lay there almost inert for several years, occasionally dumping the entire base into a new reality whenever enough energy from its nearly-empty battery crawled through a decaying circuit.

and then finally one day, there was a tiny barely-audible POP and the device finally burned out with one last spasmodic surge of timeline-crossing.

The Stahlmutter never noticed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure if this counts as a necro, and if so I'm sorry, but I wanted to comment on one thing I feel people are missing. While as many people have noted there's a seeming sort of cognitive dissonance in feeling bad for the NaziBot, it's not all that strange. The real tragedy is that the Nazis created something wonderful in this SCP; a real, functioning automaton able to respond to different circumstances (albeit through preprogrammed behaviors). This is hard to do even now, though it's mostly movement, not personality, that's the issue.

They created this thing and then they programmed it with a heaping pile of lies; the same goes for the children, in a way, although they never got the 'chance', if you can call it that, to be programmed. In that sense, it's a wonderful sort of commentary on how humans are prone to corrupting the things we create for selfish, stupid reasons, even the most extraordinary and ground-breaking creations. It's part of the greater-scope tragedy of Nazi tech in general, really, and I'm talking about real-life stuff here. The Third Reich built all kinds of fantastic things and used them for disgusting and horrifying purposes. They weaponized human innovation and curiosity against, in many cases, people who were entirely helpless.

The degeneration of possibly the best of human faculties is what's truly horrifying to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A theory - the Stahlmutter herself destroyed the other discs and possibly caused all the other damage to the base accidentally in the process of preventing her creators from stopping her attempt at doing so. She's smarter than her creators intended, smart enough to realize that if her disc is swapped out, she'll die or at least become dormant unless it gets reinstalled. Her body will keep functioning, but she wouldn't be the controlling personality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might just be a sucker for Antartic Nazi Superscience bases, but I really liked this one. There is something very melancholy about this, though I am surprised there wasn't more mentioned about the collapse of the base, or at least a few included suicide notes. One thing that slightly puzzled me was the mention that the base had been inhabited as recently as 1955, but that the site was shown to be featureless as recently as 2005. Is there supposed to be some kind of "this was transposed from an alt. reality/dimension" element here, or are we just supposed to understand that only The Foundation could uncover this? It seems a little unclear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems odd to keep some terms in german and have the translations for those words in the footnotes. Are the untranslated versions really that important? Also, having all the footnotes saying "literally [translation]" gets very repetitive. It would be better if the footnotes just had the translation, if they really need to be there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
notice that it keeps re-using the same obsolete set of sensor data.

Is it doing that because it has to?

or because it wants to?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The proper plural of "Glasgebärmutter" would be "Glasgebärmütter", though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it apparently stores programs on record-like disks, and its implied that it cannot take any actions beyond the programming on that disk. If it cannot surpass or amend the programming it is running on, then it can't really be self-aware,

This. If it ever had the potential to grow beyond it's programming, whatever seed of a mind it had has long since given way to a closed pattern of behavior.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How sure are you that it "isn't truly sapient"? If it wasn't sapient, would it have commented on the fact that the babies lie unmoving at the bottom of their tanks?

It also recognizes what death is, but claims that it doesn't apply to the clearly-dead experiments since they are neither "Untermenschen" or soldiers. Stillness indicates death. Death occurs only to Untermenschen or soldiers. The experiments are neither. Therefore, they are not dead, and the stillness is ultimately irrelevant for diagnostic purposes.

This is a very rigid if-then line of logic that we typically see in computers and machines, and it commenting on the stillness may be an attempt to "correct" what is clearly a misdiagnosis from another doctor. Outside of being completely grief-stricken or otherwise disconnected from reality, a human would immediately see the flaw in the if-then logic line, and at the very least not dismiss the apparant contradiction out-of-hand.

Also, remember that it apparently stores programs on record-like disks, and its implied that it cannot take any actions beyond the programming on that disk. If it cannot surpass or amend the programming it is running on, then it can't really be self-aware, especially if it acts as a paperweight without a program (which seems to be the case here).

If it is sapient, bringing attention to the fact that it taking care of things that have long-since died could have snapped it out of its current mode of thought. Either that, or it's completely batshit insane and trying to cling onto the one semblance of normalcy it has, which isn't really all that well-supported in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How sure are you that it "isn't truly sapient"? If it wasn't sapient, would it have commented on the fact that the babies lie unmoving at the bottom of their tanks?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you hit roughly what you were aiming for with me, or maybe a bit above it.

The feeling I get from the whole Nazi thing to begin with is a sort of quiet anger and sorrow at human stupidity; it was all so… pointless. A lot of sound and fury and pain, for nothing but a mountain of lies. This plays an interesting riff off of that: A nearly mindless thing spouting retoric and delusion that it sincerely believes, damned to repeat it until it finally falls apart. Like I said before, the same sort of pity you feel for a rabid animal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In that respect, the article succeeded, at least for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This actually has a very strong sense of Uncanny Valley and sadness. It reminds me of the Dwemer ruins in Skyrim. All the inhabitants dead, but the machines live on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: look, when you've got an anomalous artisan making magic miracle machines for you, you can't always force him to build exactly what you want. He wants to build a mechanical man, he builds a mechanical man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing I was aiming for was that cognitive dissonance between "oh god, this is so sad" and "OH THANK GOD IT FAILED". The clash between "it knits! it sings lullabies! And the babies will never be born!" and "it talks about how the Master Race will rule the world, and the Lesser Races will only die".

Is the extent to which you feel sorry for it, outdone by the extent to which you're horrified by it?

Note that "political officer" was disk #2, while "steel mother" was only #11.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article avoids the various pitfalls and cliches of magic nazi tech (except for the glowing swastika) but doesn't push forward to something really good, either. I believe I understand what this article is supposed to make me feel, but sixty million people died - it's hard to feel pathos for 12 who were never born. That said, this could be really strong with more payoff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article feels pretty weak, and there are certain writing choices that I didn't like, such as: why do you emphasize so much in using german made up words and footnoting their meaning? While it might be fun to see it, it broke my suspension of desbelief when I realized the Nazi robot was actually speaking English, because there's no way a competent translator wouldn't translate those words and footnote its original german nature. Also, adding "literally" to begin every footnote is repetitive.

Overall, the topic and supporting ideas were just overused tropes1, and there was no actual hook for me. After thinking about it, this felt like a weird mix of Civil war and Age of Ultron, with a twist.

-1.

Footnotes
1. Nazi era anomalous stuff, quasi sapient robot, people growing in glass tubes, Antartica base, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Scorpion451 and A Vacuum have already summed up my feelings on this one nicely, +1

My only nitpick is the swastika shaped power source. That sounds a little goofy. Perhaps its power source could be visible through a swastika shaped window?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only thought upon reading this was: "what if the robot is right (the kids are still alive) and the Foundation is wrong?" I +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly where the pity comes in. There's no satisfying "punching Hitler" stuff here, no lost nazi supertech. Just a broken robot and a bunch of failed experiments. And nothing beside remains.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sometimes, when you see the ruins of something that's been consigned to history and forgotten, the overwhelming reaction is "good." Except the central character of this piece, as Reject noted above, isn't even enough of a character that I can take pleasure at its failure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me, it's not about the robot never getting to see it's little abominations born. It's closer to the feeling I get looking at pictures of Pripyat…a sort of "Look on my works, ye mighty, and despair." thing mixed with the sort of pity you feel for a rabid animal. The fact that it isn't truely sapient actually makes it more pathetic to me, to be honest- it doesn't know anything but what that disk tells it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Antarctica Nazis are always preferable to Illinois Nazis. The ongoing fascination with Third Reich superscience and Wunderwaffe is a strange cultural phenomenon. I blame Pauwels and Bergier; "Morning of the Magicians" introduced an entire generation to it.

This doesn't seem to inspire sympathy for this unlikely and unlikeable AI, at least for me. Unlike a number of other SCPs, this seems too primitive to possess sentience, which is my benchmark for sympathy. The Brave Little Toaster was more of a character.

It does make me wonder what the point of the project was. Human beings can be produced relatively cheaply using unskilled labor, and they already had the Lebensborn homes. Perhaps there was something special about these particular bottle babies?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The glowing swastika bit is just…

Urgh. Doofy. Kinda spoils the otherwise restrained (by anomaNazi standards anyway) mood of the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Me too, except I still don't. This article hinges on the reader sympathizing with a robot failing its quest to rear nazi super soldiers. I want the robot to fail. Moreover, I don't empathize with it at all because it has no characterization other than that it is a nazi robot (and that it is maternal… but since it could theoretically become a nutritionist at the flick of a switch, that aspect feels less authentic).

Also, it's powered by a glowing swastika. Are you kidding me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You had me at Ersatzfruchtwasser. I love dessicated Nazi babies - just add water!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Normally I'm all up for nazis getting totally fucking owned, even if it's just in the form of an automaton doing useless work for babies who will never be born, but I worry that that is not going to be the popular takeaway from this article, and moreover it's just not clicking with me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The "Nazi superscience" and "plight of artificial intelligence" angles are ones that I can see people enjoying, though I've been tired of magical Nazi tech for quite a long time now. I've never quite been comfortable with the subconscious admiration for the technological prowess of the Third Reich that comes with that sort of thing, even though that's not what's intended in 99% of depictions.

What's really making this not work for me is the fact that this hinges on a feeling of pity and sympathy for the automaton, which is locked in a routine in which it apparently feels overwhelming nurturing and caring emotions for something that will never come into being. There's nothing inherently wrong with going for that sort of story. But I personally am having a very difficult time being all that sad that the SS Class of 1971 will never come to pass. More than that, I am having a difficult time caring that a robot, programmed with Nazi attitudes about "untermenschen" and all the attendant racial purity nonsense that comes with that, will never know the joy of raising a nursery full of baby Aryan warriors. And I get the strong impression that I am supposed to care about that (as apparently a lot of people do here).

This isn't a commentary on the author, who I'm quite sure does not hold any sympathy for the political system in which this anomaly operates. But robots who want nothing more than to build up the Master Race, complete with Tony Stark glowing swastikas in their chests, make a poor subject if what I'm supposed to feel is anything other than contempt for the character. Without that emotional connection, this is just another entry in a trope that I don't have a whole lot of interest in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this was a neat article with a touching interview, and then comment got me laughing so hard XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This such a sad and melancholy article. I find myself having a lot of sympathy for this little nazi robot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For sale: Nazi baby sweaters, never worn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reads like something you'd find in a Fallout game. Intriguing, short and sweet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. Never thought I'd find myself feeling sorry for a nazi robot.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nevermind.
